Snipara’s core value proposition is Context Optimization as a Service: it drastically reduces large documentation into a small, highly relevant context (≈90% reduction—from ~500K tokens to ~5K), fits that context to a client-specified token budget, and returns optimized context (with metadata) so the client can use their own LLM. Key benefits: LLM-agnostic/no vendor lock-in, lower costs (no LLM inference passed through), and high margins.

Key benefits:
- ~90% context reduction (e.g., 500K → ~5K tokens)
- LLM‑agnostic (clients use their own models via MCP)
- Low cost / high margins (compute only, no LLM API fees)
- Fast, optimized retrieval (search, scoring, chunking, token budgeting)

Snipara is split into three cooperating parts so the context-optimization logic is reusable, scalable, and easy to integrate:

1. Client (snipara-mcp)
- The user-facing SDK/CLI installed via pip or uvx (local package under apps/mcp-server/snipara-mcp).
- Exposes the MCP tools (rlm_context_query, rlm_ask, rlm_search, etc.) and talks to the backend over HTTP or stdio.
- Used for documentation queries and as the primary integration point for apps and agent clients (Claude, GPT, Windsurf, etc.).

2. MCP Server (snipara-fastapi)
- FastAPI backend that implements auth, storage, the context-optimization engine (rlm_engine), and the MCP transport layer.
- Source lives under apps/mcp-server/src/ in the monorepo and must be mirrored to the separate snipara-fastapi repo (src/) — key files: server.py, auth.py, rlm_engine.py, mcp_transport.py.
- Deployed as the API (api.snipara.com) and serves the MCP endpoints used by clients.

3. Platform / Web & Deployment layer
- Hosting and orchestration: Vercel for the web app (frontend) and Railway for the MCP server (with Postgres and Redis).
- Distribution and CI: PyPI for the client package, two GitHub repos (monorepo → RLMSaas for Vercel/PyPI and snipara-fastapi for Railway) with a mandatory dual-repo sync workflow and recommended manual Railway deployment (`railway up`).
- Configuration and discovery (e.g., .mcp.json) tie clients to the deployed MCP endpoints.

Why this split matters: the client SDK stays lightweight and portable, the server centralizes heavy context-optimization and auth, and the platform layer handles scaling, persistence, and public exposure — making Snipara LLM-agnostic and easy to integrate.

Snipara is built as a simple three-component architecture. At a high level those components are:

1) Client / SDK layer
- What it is: the snipara-mcp client, CLI/agent integrations, or any MCP-compatible client (Claude Desktop, Cursor, Windsurf, GPT, custom agents).
- Role: invoke Snipara tools and request optimized context. Clients use the MCP protocol (HTTP or stdio) to call tools like rlm_context_query, rlm_ask, rlm_search, etc.
- Key notes: distributed to users via pip (snipara-mcp) or uvx; configured by .mcp.json (HTTP or stdio endpoints).

2) MCP Server (backend)
- What it is: the FastAPI server (snipara-fastapi / apps/mcp-server/src/) that implements the MCP HTTP endpoints, tool handlers and the context-optimization engine (rlm_engine).
- Role: receive MCP requests, authenticate them, run the context-optimization and tool workflows, fetch/store documents, and return compact, budgeted context for LLMs.
- Components: server.py (FastAPI entry), auth.py (API key / OAuth), mcp_transport.py (HTTP transport layer), rlm_engine.py (optimization logic), DB/usage layers (Postgres/Redis).
- Deployment: typically deployed to Railway (api.snipara.com). Important operational notes: dual-repo sync required when changing server code (copy files to snipara-fastapi repo and push), and use manual Railway CLI deploy (railway up) because Git auto-deploy is flaky.

3) Web/Management & Infrastructure
- What it is: the public web app/dashboard and the backing infra services.
- Role: user-facing web app (Vercel) for docs/dashboard, auth flows and UX; infrastructure (Postgres, Redis) for storage/state/cache; CI/publishing flows (PyPI, Vercel, Railway).
- Deployment & ops: web app hosted on Vercel (pnpm build, apps/web/.next), MCP backend on Railway (Postgres + Redis). Releases to PyPI/pkgs produced from the monorepo packaging flow.

How they work together (data flow)
- A client issues an MCP call (HTTP or stdio) to the MCP Server.
- The server authenticates the request, runs rlm_engine and various tools (semantic search, token budgeting, etc.), reads/writes documents in Postgres/Redis, and returns a compacted context or tool response.
- The web/dashboard provides UI, documentation and admin flows; infra services persist data and support scaling.

Developer & operational reminders
- Always try the MCP (rlm_context_query / rlm_ask) first for documentation/code lookups (dogfooding).
- When changing server code in apps/mcp-server/src, push to both repos (RLMSaas and snipara-fastapi) — dual-repo push is mandatory.
- For Railway deploys use: cd snipara-fastapi && railway up (manual).
- Example client config: .mcp.json points to https://api.snipara.com/mcp/snipara with an X-API-Key header.

This split keeps client integrations lightweight, concentrates optimization/tool logic in a single backend service, and separates user-facing UI and persistence/infrastructure for scalability and operations.

Short version
- Snipara enforces a strict token budget when assembling context for an LLM call: count tokens, pick the most relevant content until the budget is exhausted, and smart-truncate the last piece if needed.

How it works (details)
- Token counting
  - Uses tiktoken (cl100k_base encoder) to count tokens accurately (not characters or words).
  - tiktoken is required in the MCP server (pyproject dependency tiktoken>=0.5.0).

- Primary tool: rlm_context_query
  - Parameters: query, max_tokens (token budget, default 4000), search_mode (keyword|semantic|hybrid), include_metadata, optional session_id.
  - Response includes a sections array with per-section metadata (title, content, file path, line ranges, relevance_score, token_count, truncated flag), total_tokens, truncated (overall), session_context, and suggestions.

- Selection algorithm
  - Candidate sections are scored (keyword/semantic/hybrid search + ranking).
  - Greedy selection: add highest-relevance sections first.
  - Stop when adding the next full section would exceed max_tokens.
  - If a partial section fits, run smart_truncate() to fit the remaining tokens and mark that section as truncated.

- Smart truncation
  - Truncate text to fit remaining tokens using the same encoder.
  - Prefer sentence-boundary cuts (split on . ! ?); do not cut mid-sentence when possible.
  - Add an ellipsis ("...") and set truncated: true for that section.

- Session context & system instructions
  - Previous session context can be injected; by default it is limited (implementation note: session context allowed up to ~20% of budget).
  - System instructions may be auto-injected as part of the budget calculations.

- Metadata & transparency
  - Each returned section includes file path, start/end lines, relevance score, and token_count.
  - The top-level response reports total_tokens used, whether the result was truncated, and suggestions for other docs to consult.

- Budget allocation patterns
  - Snipara supports category-based allocations (examples in code):
    - A shared-category example: MANDATORY 40%, BEST_PRACTICES 30%, GUIDELINES 20%, REFERENCE 10%.
    - A project/shared split example: project ~40% plus shared categories (MANDATORY, BEST_PRACTICES, ...).
  - The web-side allocation logic groups docs (project vs shared categories), computes per-category budgets from the total, and allocates within each bucket by relevance until that bucket’s budget is used.

- Other behaviors & tools
  - Recursive helpers: decompose queries into sub-queries and estimate per-sub-query token needs.
  - multi_query: run multiple context queries in parallel with per-query budgets and aggregate tokens used.
  - Hard limits and safeguards: limits on tokens per collection, docs per collection, and minimum relevance thresholds are enforced.

Why this matters
- Accurate token accounting + greedy relevance selection maximizes the usefulness of the returned context while ensuring you never exceed the token budget (and therefore cost/latency constraints).
- Smart truncation keeps content readable and semantically coherent (no half-sentences).
- Category/project allocations and session-awareness let teams prioritize the most important docs automatically.

- Show an example rlm_context_query request/response with token counts, or
- Walk through how to tune category allocations for your project’s docs. Which would be most helpful?

Short answer
- Snipara measures and enforces a hard token budget when assembling context for an LLM prompt. It counts tokens with tiktoken (cl100k_base), ranks candidate sections by relevance, and greedily includes the highest‑relevance sections until the token budget is exhausted. If only part of a section fits, it smart‑truncates at a sentence boundary and marks that section as truncated.

How it works (step‑by‑step)
1. Token counting
   - Uses tiktoken (cl100k_base) to count tokens for each candidate section and for the session/system context.
   - Token counts drive every selection decision.

2. Search & candidate generation
   - Supports search_mode: keyword, semantic, or hybrid.
   - Produces candidate sections with metadata (file path, start/end lines, raw content).

3. Ranking
   - Candidates are ranked by a relevance score (from keyword/semantic matching and fusion for hybrid).

4. Greedy selection within the budget
   - Given max_tokens (default 4000), the engine iterates ranked sections in order:
     - If a full section fits, include it.
     - If a full section would exceed the remaining budget, attempt a partial include:
       - Use smart_truncate() to cut at the nearest sentence boundary that fits the remaining tokens.
       - Mark that section as truncated and add an ellipsis ("...").
     - Stop when the budget is consumed or no more sections are available.
   - The response contains selected sections, per‑section token counts, total_tokens, and a truncated flag.

5. Smart truncation rules
   - Truncates to fit remaining tokens.
   - Cuts only at sentence boundaries (. ! ?), not mid‑sentence.
   - Appends "..." and sets a truncated indicator in the section metadata.

6. Budget allocation & priorities
   - Supports higher‑level allocation schemes (e.g., project vs shared, or category budgets like MANDATORY/BEST_PRACTICES/GUIDELINES/REFERENCE).
   - Default allocations (examples): project ~40%, MANDATORY ~30%, BEST_PRACTICES ~20%, GUIDELINES ~10%, REFERENCE ~10% (configurable).
   - Allocation logic groups docs and assigns token allowances per category, then applies greedy selection within each allocation.

7. Session & system context
   - Session context can be included (bounded, e.g., up to ~20% of the budget).
   - System instructions are auto‑injected and accounted for in the token budget.

8. Response metadata
   - Each returned section includes: title, content, file path, line range, relevance_score, token_count, and truncated flag (if applicable).
   - Top-level response fields: total_tokens, truncated, session_context (if any), suggestions (related files), and search metrics (sections_scanned, search_time_ms).

Extras / advanced
- Tools for decomposition and parallel multi‑query runs exist; they accept per‑query budgets and aggregate token accounting.
- The system includes tests and Pydantic schemas (ContextQueryParams, ContextSection, ContextQueryResult) and is wired into the tool router as rlm_context_query.

Example request/response (conceptual)
Request:
{ "tool":"rlm_context_query", "params": { "query":"How does authentication work?", "max_tokens":4000, "search_mode":"hybrid", "include_metadata":true } }

Response summary:
- sections: top relevant passages up to 4000 tokens (smart‑truncated if needed)
- total_tokens: actual tokens returned
- truncated: true/false
- session_context: prior context (if included)
- suggestions: related docs to check

Where this is implemented
- tiktoken usage and new tool: apps/mcp-server/src/rlm_engine.py (rlm_context_query)
- New Pydantic models: apps/mcp-server/src/models.py
- pyproject.toml updated to include tiktoken
- Tests added: apps/mcp-server/tests/test_context_query.py

- Explain how to tune category allocations for a particular use case. Which would you prefer?

Short answer
- Snipara enforces a token budget by counting tokens with tiktoken (cl100k_base), ranking candidate context sections by relevance, and greedily selecting the highest‑relevance sections until the token budget is filled. If a section only partially fits, it is smart‑truncated at a sentence boundary and flagged as truncated.

Key concepts (what actually happens)
- Tokens: Snipara counts tokens with tiktoken (cl100k_base) so counts match GPT‑4/Claude encodings instead of naive character/word estimates.
- Budget: The caller specifies a max_tokens budget (default examples use 4000). The engine must return context whose summed token_count ≤ budget (except when a last partial section is truncated to fit).
- Relevance ranking: Candidate sections are scored (keyword/semantic/hybrid search); selection is greedy by descending relevance score.
- Smart truncation: If remaining tokens don’t fit a full section, Snipara uses smart_truncate() to cut at the last sentence boundary (. ! ?) that fits the remaining tokens, appends an ellipsis marker, and marks that section as truncated.
- Metadata: Returned sections include file path, line range, relevance_score, token_count and a truncated flag when applicable. The top-level result includes total_tokens used, truncated boolean, session_context (if any), and suggestions.

Allocation strategies and policies
- Category allocation: For shared docs Snipara supports priority/category budgets (example default allocations: project 40%, MANDATORY 25%, BEST_PRACTICES 20%, GUIDELINES 10%, REFERENCE 5%). Project docs are prioritized.
- Hard limits: There are enforced hard limits (e.g., max tokens per collection) and a minimum relevance threshold to ignore low‑value docs.
- Session context and system instructions: The engine can include recent session context (bounded, e.g., up to ~20% of the budget) and will auto‑inject system instructions as needed.

Tooling & API
- Primary tool: rlm_context_query(query, max_tokens=4000, search_mode="keyword|semantic|hybrid", include_metadata=True). Response contains a "sections" list + total_tokens, truncated flag, session_context, suggestions.
- Internal flow: perform search (mode selectable), fuse/rank candidates, encode and count tokens with tiktoken, greedily select sections until budget exhausted, smart_truncate last partial section if needed, synthesize suggestions.
- Multi-query support: There are helpers to decompose complex queries into subqueries and to run multiple context queries in parallel with per‑query budgets.

Metrics & efficiency
- Token efficiency metrics (compression_ratio, token_reduction_pct, cost_savings_pct) measure how much Snipara reduces tokens compared to returning full documents. This is computed without calling an LLM (pure token accounting).
- Tool responses also surface search_time_ms, sections_scanned, and cache_hit info for observability.

Where it’s implemented (code pointers)
- Token counting and context selection logic live in the MCP server rlm engine (apps/mcp-server/src/rlm_engine.py) and use tiktoken; Pydantic models (ContextQueryParams, ContextSection, ContextQueryResult) are defined in models.py; tests added under apps/mcp-server/tests/test_context_query.py.

Example flow (simple)
1. Client calls rlm_context_query(query="How does authentication work?", max_tokens=4000, search_mode="hybrid").  
2. Engine searches and ranks candidate sections.  
3. Encoder counts tokens per section.  
4. Engine selects top sections until tokens would exceed 4000.  
5. If the last section is too large, it is smart-truncated at a sentence boundary and flagged.  
6. Engine returns selected sections with file/line metadata, total_tokens, truncated flag, session_context and suggestions.

Shared context collections are team- or organization-level bundles of reusable rules, standards, templates and reference material (coding standards, architecture rules, code-review templates, etc.) that are created once by admins and automatically surfaced to every project and developer on the team so the team’s AI-driven workflows stay consistent.

How they work (summary)
- Create and upload
  - A team admin creates a Shared Context Collection and uploads documents (rlm_upload_shared_document) such as MANDATORY rules, BEST_PRACTICES, GUIDELINES, and REFERENCE material.
  - Documents include a category and a priority (0–100) to control importance within a category.
- Link to projects
  - Collections are linked to projects via the dashboard. Linked projects automatically inherit the collection’s documents.
- Retrieval and tooling
  - rlm_shared_context merges linked collections and returns categorized documents.
  - rlm_list_templates and rlm_get_template expose reusable prompt templates stored in collections.
  - rlm_context_query (and RLM runtime calls) will automatically include shared context when querying project docs.
- Injection & session use
  - You can inject shared context into a session (rlm_inject) so every subsequent query must follow the team standards.
- Auto-enforcement and query order
  - Shared context is included before local project documents in queries (shared-first). The token budget between shared context and project docs is configurable (examples use 30–40% for shared context and the remainder for project docs).
  - Within the shared context payload, categories are allocated token budgets so higher-priority content is favored first.

Categories & token priorities (how content is prioritized)
- MANDATORY — Highest priority; non-negotiable rules (security, architecture). (Example allocation: 40% of shared context tokens.)
- BEST_PRACTICES — High priority; recommended patterns (example: 30%).
- GUIDELINES — Medium priority; helpful suggestions (example: 20%).
- REFERENCE — Low priority; background info and examples (example: 10%).

Typical workflow example
- Admin uploads “TypeScript Coding Standards” (MANDATORY and BEST_PRACTICES).
- Developer starts a session: call rlm_shared_context(categories=[…]) and rlm_inject to load standards.
- All subsequent rlm_context_query or RLM completion calls automatically include those standards; code generation and reviews must follow them.
- Decisions can be recorded (rlm_remember) for future auditability.

When to use / benefits
- Enforce security and architectural rules consistently across projects.
- Ensure consistent code style, testing patterns, naming conventions.
- Provide pre-built templates for code review, PR checks, and feature specs.
- Search and discover existing implementations across all team projects (rlm_multi_project_query).

Access & limits
- Shared context collections and multi-project features require the Team plan (or Enterprise).
- Collections can be public, team-scoped or global; admins control linking and usage.

Best practices for collections
- Start with MANDATORY rules (security/architecture).
- Add BEST_PRACTICES gradually; use priority scores to rank importance.
- Keep each document focused on one topic.
- Update collections as standards evolve and use templates for consistency.

If you want, I can walk through creating a sample shared collection for your team (fields/structure and example documents) or show the exact API calls to upload and link one.

Shared context collections are team-shared libraries of reusable rules, standards, templates, and reference docs that get automatically included in queries for any project that links to the collection. They let teams enforce consistent coding standards, security rules, architecture guidance, and review templates so every developer — and every assistant session — uses the same conventions.

How they work (overview)
- Create a collection (team admin) and upload documents (coding standards, best practices, templates).
- Link that collection to one or more projects in the dashboard. Linked projects automatically inherit the collection.
- When you run queries, the system merges shared context with project docs and returns the combined context to the model so responses follow team rules.

Key mechanics
- Categorized documents: MANDATORY, BEST_PRACTICES, GUIDELINES, REFERENCE.
  - Categories have a token budget allocation (typical default): MANDATORY 40%, BEST_PRACTICES 30%, GUIDELINES 20%, REFERENCE 10%.
  - Each document also has a priority (0–100) to rank importance within its category.
- Query order: shared context is loaded first (a configurable portion of the token budget), then project-specific documents use the remainder. This ensures team rules are considered before local docs.
- Tools/APIs:
  - rlm_shared_context — fetch merged shared context (filters like categories, max_tokens).
  - rlm_list_templates / rlm_get_template — list or fetch prompt templates stored in collections.
  - rlm_context_query — queries that automatically include shared context when enabled.
  - rlm_inject — inject shared context into a session so it applies to subsequent queries.

Typical workflows
- Admin: create collection, upload MANDATORY rules + best practices, link to projects.
- Developer/session start: call rlm_shared_context(categories=[...]) and rlm_inject() to put team rules into the session.
- During implementation: rlm_context_query or model completions will include shared context so generated code and decisions follow the team standards.
- Team visibility: templates for code reviews, multi-project search, and remembering decisions help enforce and surface standards.

Benefits
- Consistency across projects and developers
- Automatic enforcement of non-negotiable rules (security, API validation)
- Centralized updates (change once, all linked projects inherit)
- Reusable templates for reviews and specs

Access & limits
- Team/Enterprise plans required for team-wide shared collections (not available on Free/Pro).
- Collections can be public or private and track usageCount; priorities and categories control how much influence a document has in responses.

In short: shared context collections let teams centrally define and automatically inject standards, patterns, and templates into every relevant query, giving predictable, team-aligned AI outputs across projects.

Shared Context Collections are team-level, reusable collections of documents (coding standards, architecture rules, best practices, templates, examples) that are automatically made available to all projects linked to the collection. They make it possible to enforce and share consistent team rules so every developer — and every AI query run against project docs — follows the same conventions.

How they work (high-level)
- Create a collection (Team admin) and upload documents describing rules, patterns, guidelines, and reference material.
- Link the collection to one or more projects in the dashboard. Linked projects automatically inherit the collection’s documents.
- When you run queries (rlm_context_query, RLM completions, etc.), the shared context is merged with project docs and injected into the prompt so the assistant always sees team standards before project-specific content.
- Shared context documents are prioritized and budgeted so high‑priority rules are always included.

Key concepts and behavior
- Categories (with priority/token budget):
  - MANDATORY — Highest priority; non‑negotiable rules (40% token budget).
  - BEST_PRACTICES — Recommended patterns (30%).
  - GUIDELINES — Helpful suggestions (20%).
  - REFERENCE — Background examples/explanations (10%).
- Within a category you can use a numeric priority (0–100) to influence importance/order of documents.
- MCP query order: shared context is loaded first (default ~30% of the token budget, configurable), then project docs consume the remainder. The returned results present shared context sections before local sections.
- Collections are merged from all linked team collections (e.g., team standards + React best practices) and returned by rlm_shared_context.

APIs / tools
- rlm_shared_context(...) — get merged shared documents (can filter categories and set max_tokens).
- rlm_context_query(...) — project query that automatically includes shared context (use include_shared_context and shared_context_budget_percent).
- rlm_list_templates(...) / rlm_get_template(...) — list and retrieve prompt templates stored in collections (useful for code reviews, PR checklists, etc.).
- rlm_upload_shared_document(...) — upload documents into a collection (admin action).

Typical workflow
1. Admin creates a Shared Context Collection and uploads MANDATORY rules first.
2. Link the collection to projects in the dashboard (one-time).
3. Developers call rlm_shared_context at session start (or rely on automatic injection) so subsequent queries include team rules.
4. Implement features or run code reviews; the assistant enforces MANDATORY rules and pays attention to BEST_PRACTICES and GUIDELINES.
5. Record decisions with rlm_remember for auditability.

Best practices
- Put security/architecture non‑negotiables in MANDATORY.
- Keep documents focused (one topic per doc).
- Use priorities to indicate importance within a category.
- Update collections as standards evolve and use templates for consistent reviews/specs.

Limits / access
- Team/Enterprise plans required for team‑wide shared collections (not available on Free/Pro).
- Collections can be public or private; admins control linking and usage.

In short: Shared Context Collections let teams centrally store, prioritize, and auto‑inject coding standards, best practices, and templates into every project query so humans and AI produce consistent, standards‑compliant results.

Short answer
- Snipara implements the OAuth 2.0 Device Authorization Grant (RFC 8628). The CLI starts the device flow, shows a short user code + verification URL, the user authorizes in a browser, and the CLI polls the server until an access token is issued. The token is then used as a Bearer token (Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_...) for MCP requests.

Step-by-step flow
1. CLI requests device authorization:
   - POST /api/oauth/device/code with body { "client_id": "snipara_cli" }.
2. Server response contains:
   - device_code (used by the CLI to poll),
   - user_code (displayed to the user),
   - verification_uri (URL the user visits),
   - verification_uri_complete (optional one-click URL),
   - expires_in (lifetime in seconds),
   - interval (recommended poll interval in seconds).
   Example response:
   {
     "device_code":"abc123...",
     "user_code":"ABCD-1234",
     "verification_uri":"https://snipara.com/device",
     "verification_uri_complete":"https://snipara.com/device?code=ABCD-1234",
     "expires_in":900,
     "interval":5
   }
3. User authorizes:
   - User opens verification_uri (or verification_uri_complete) in a browser, enters the user_code (or uses the complete URL) and signs in/approves the client.
4. CLI polls token endpoint:
   - The CLI polls the server at the interval using device_code until the user authorizes or the device_code expires.
5. Token received and stored:
   - On success the server returns an access token (used as Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_...).
   - Snipara CLI stores tokens (e.g., ~/.snipara/tokens.json) and will prefer OAuth tokens first for MCP requests.

Notes & troubleshooting
- Priority for MCP authentication: stored OAuth token (if present) → API key from environment.
- If OAuth is expired or you want to force API-key auth:
  - Set SNIPARA_IGNORE_OAUTH=true in the MCP client env to skip tokens and use SNIPARA_API_KEY, or
  - Remove/backup ~/.snipara/tokens.json (rm or mv) to force API-key fallback.
- If you see “OAuth expired”, re-run the device flow (CLI command) to re-authenticate.

Why use device flow
- Allows secure authentication for CLI/headless clients without pasting API keys; user authenticates in a browser.

Short answer: Snipara uses the OAuth 2.0 Device Authorization Flow (RFC 8628). The CLI starts the flow, the user completes authorization in a browser with a short code, and the CLI polls for the access token. Snipara stores the OAuth token locally and sends it as a Bearer token; if no valid OAuth token is found it falls back to API-key auth.

How it works (step‑by‑step)
1. CLI requests a device code
   - POST /api/oauth/device/code with body { "client_id": "snipara_cli" }.
   - Server responds with:
     - device_code (for the server to poll)
     - user_code (the short code the user enters)
     - verification_uri and verification_uri_complete (URL to open)
     - expires_in (seconds until code expires)
     - interval (recommended polling interval)
   - Example response (from docs):
     {
       "device_code": "abc123...",
       "user_code": "ABCD-1234",
       "verification_uri": "https://snipara.com/device",
       "verification_uri_complete": "https://snipara.com/device?code=ABCD-1234",
       "expires_in": 900,
       "interval": 5
     }

2. CLI shows the user_code + verification URL (or verification_uri_complete) and asks the user to open it and sign in.

3. User authenticates in the browser and approves the device using the user_code.

4. CLI polls the token endpoint using the device_code at the specified interval until:
   - Server returns the access token (success),
   - The code expires, or
   - The user denies/ cancels (error).
   (This follows RFC 8628 polling semantics — poll interval and expires_in control behavior.)

5. Token storage and use
   - On success the client stores the OAuth tokens (access/refresh) locally (docs reference ~/.snipara/tokens.json).
   - Requests use Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_... when an OAuth token is present.

6. Fallback and priority
   - Snipara first checks for a stored OAuth token (~/.snipara/tokens.json).
   - If no valid OAuth token is present it falls back to API key authentication (X-API-Key: rlm_...).

Troubleshooting / tips
- To force API-key authentication instead of OAuth, set SNIPARA_IGNORE_OAUTH=true in the client env (recommended) or remove/backup ~/.snipara/tokens.json (rm or mv).
- OAuth header format: Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_...
- API key header format: X-API-Key: rlm_...
- If OAuth token expires, re-run the device flow to re-authenticate.
- Device flow avoids copying API keys — good for CLI/desktop apps.

If you want, I can show an example CLI interaction or an example polling loop for the token endpoint.

Short answer
- Snipara uses the OAuth Device Flow (RFC 8628) so CLI users can authenticate in a browser without copying API keys.
- The CLI starts the device flow, the user opens a URL and enters a short user code (or clicks a verification URL), and the CLI polls the server until an access token is issued. The token is then stored locally and used as a Bearer token for MCP requests.

How it works (step‑by‑step)
1. CLI requests a device code
   - POST /api/oauth/device/code with body: { "client_id": "snipara_cli" }.
2. Server response includes:
   - device_code (opaque code for the client)
   - user_code (short code the user types in)
   - verification_uri (URL the user visits)
   - verification_uri_complete (URL that already includes the code)
   - expires_in (lifetime, e.g. 900s)
   - interval (recommended polling interval, e.g. 5s)
3. User completes authentication in browser
   - User visits verification_uri (or clicks verification_uri_complete) and signs in/authorizes the CLI using the user_code shown by the CLI.
4. CLI polls for the token
   - The CLI polls the token endpoint at the given interval until the device flow completes or the device_code expires. Once authorized, the server returns an access token.
5. Token storage and usage
   - The issued OAuth token is saved locally (e.g., ~/.snipara/tokens.json) and used in requests as:
     Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_...
   - Snipara’s auth priority: check stored OAuth token first, fall back to API key.

Headers / fallback
- OAuth header: Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_...
- API key header: X-API-Key: rlm_...
- If you want to force API-key auth, either set SNIPARA_IGNORE_OAUTH=true in the MCP client env or remove/backup ~/.snipara/tokens.json.

Notes / troubleshooting
- The response’s expires_in and interval tell the client how long the code is valid and how often to poll.
- If OAuth expires or fails, the CLI can fall back to an API key (or you can re-run the device flow).

Short answer — the core/primary Prisma models in Snipara are:

- User
- Team (and TeamMember)
- Project (and project membership/access models)
- Document (with versions)
- ApiKey (and TeamApiKey)
- AuditLog / audit_logs

Plus the new shared‑context models added for reusable content/tools:

- SharedContextCollection
- SharedDocument
- SharedMcpConfig
- PromptTemplate
- ProjectSharedContext

Notes:
- The canonical schema lives at packages/database/prisma/schema.prisma.
- Prisma models use PascalCase singular names and are mapped to snake_case plural table names.

Main models (core + notable domain models) used in Snipara:

- User — application user/account.
- Team — organization/team grouping users and projects.
- TeamMember — membership record linking a User to a Team (role, permissions).
- Project — project/workspace under a team.
- Document — project documents/content (with versions/history).
- DocumentVersion — historical versions of a document (used for restore/versioning).
- ProjectMember / Project access models — project-level access records (editor/viewer/admin).
- ApiKey / TeamApiKey — API keys scoped to a project or to a team.
- AuditLog — audit entries (action, actor, entity, timestamps, metadata).

Shared context / content models (new additions in the schema):
- SharedContextCollection — a collection of reusable/shared context (scope: GLOBAL/TEAM/USER).
- SharedDocument — individual shared documents inside a collection.
- SharedMcpConfig — tool/configuration settings attached to a collection.
- PromptTemplate — reusable prompt templates in a collection.
- ProjectSharedContext — junction table linking Projects to SharedContextCollections (with per-link config).

These are the primary models referenced in the provided schema and API docs.

The schema centers on a few core entities plus a set of shared-context models. Main database models are:

- User — application users.
- Team / TeamMember — teams and their membership records (TeamMember used in Prisma).
- Project — projects owned by teams/users.
- ApiKey / TeamApiKey — API keys (team-scoped keys are TeamApiKey).
- AuditLog (audit_logs) — audit log entries (exists in production).
- SharedContextCollection — a collection of shared context (templates/docs) with scope (GLOBAL/TEAM/USER).
- SharedDocument — documents inside a SharedContextCollection.
- SharedMcpConfig — tool/configuration settings for a collection.
- PromptTemplate — prompt templates stored in a collection.
- ProjectSharedContext — junction table linking Projects to SharedContextCollections (per-project config/overrides).

(Prisma model names are singular PascalCase in the schema — e.g., User, Project, TeamMember, SharedContextCollection, SharedDocument, SharedMcpConfig, PromptTemplate, ProjectSharedContext.)

Snipara uses a strict layered (n-tier / clean-style) architecture with one-way imports:

- Top: Routes (apps/web/src/app/api/) — thin HTTP handlers
- Services (lib/services/) — external API adapters, lazy-init + retry
- Repositories (lib/db/queries/) — centralized CRUD / DB access (only place to call Prisma)
- Bottom: Utils & Types — pure helpers and types with no dependencies

Key rules: lower layers cannot import from higher layers; route handlers must authenticate, validate (Zod), delegate to services/queries, and return handled responses.

  ↓ imports
- Services (lib/services/) — external API adapters, lazy init + retry
  ↓ imports

  ↓ imports
- Utils & Types — pure shared code with no dependencies

Snipara uses a strict layered architecture (a clean/onsion-style layering) with a one-way import hierarchy:

- app/api/ (Routes) — thin HTTP handlers (authenticate → validate → delegate → respond)  
- lib/services/ — external API adapters / business integrations  
- lib/db/queries/ — repository layer (all DB CRUD and permission checks; no direct Prisma in routes)  
- types/ & utils/ — shared, pure helpers with NO dependencies

Key rules: lower layers cannot import from higher layers; all DB access goes through repositories; services use lazy init/retry patterns; route handlers must remain thin and delegate to services/queries.

Short answer
- Snipara compacts your 500K-token codebase into a small, highly relevant context (typically ~5K tokens) that fits easily inside a 128K LLM window. That gives you dramatically lower token usage per LLM call, much lower latency, and far lower LLM billing.
- For documents larger than the LLM window, Snipara’s recursive tools (rlm_decompose / rlm_multi_query) let the client LLM orchestrate multiple budgeted queries and synthesize results — effectively enabling “near‑infinite” context while still minimizing cost.

How it helps (mechanics)
- Context reduction: Snipara finds and returns only the most relevant sections (example: 90%+ reduction: 500K → ~5K tokens).
- Token budgeting & smart truncation: You set max_tokens and Snipara greedily fills the budget, truncating at sentence boundaries if needed.
- Shared context & ordering: Shared team rules can be included first (configurable percent), so you don’t resend large common docs every call.
- Session persistence: Session context can survive compaction (up to configured percent of budget), reducing repeated resends.
- No LLM compute costs: Snipara doesn’t run inference — you use your LLM and pay only for the tokens you send/receive.

Concrete cost comparisons (simple math)
- Basic formula: cost = (tokens_sent + tokens_returned) / 1000 * price_per_1k
- Relative token reduction:
  - 500,000 tokens → 5,000 tokens is a 100× reduction (99% fewer tokens).
  - Compared to a 128K window, 5K is only ~3.9% of the window.

Example scenarios (hypothetical prices)
1) If your LLM charges $0.02 per 1K tokens:
   - Sending 500K tokens (naive) → (500,000/1,000)*$0.02 = $10.00
   - Sending 5K tokens (Snipara result) → (5,000/1,000)*$0.02 = $0.10
   - Savings ≈ $9.90 per call (99% reduction).

2) If your LLM charges $0.005 per 1K tokens:
   - 500K → $2.50 ; 5K → $0.025 ; savings ≈ $2.475 per call.

3) If you need to cover the whole 500K with a 128K LLM:
   - Without Snipara you’d need multiple large calls or expensive pre‑processing. With Snipara’s recursive decomposition you make multiple small, budgeted calls focused on sub-queries. Total tokens used will usually be far less than blindly resending full docs because each sub-call is limited to relevant slices.

Other practical savings
- Fewer follow-ups: richer metadata (file, lines, relevance score) reduces back-and-forth.
- Lower latency: smaller payloads → faster network + faster model processing.
- Team-scale efficiency: shared collections eliminate repeated inclusion of the same team rules across projects.

What to configure for best results
- Set max_tokens to the portion of your 128K window you want to dedicate to context (e.g., 100K for context + 28K for prompt/output).
- Choose shared_context_budget_percent (default example: 30%) so team rules are included first.
- Use rlm_decompose + rlm_multi_query for complex, multi-part queries spanning >128K.
- Use session context to avoid re-sending unchanged team/project conventions.

Bottom line
Snipara turns a 500K-token codebase into a few thousand highly relevant tokens per query (typical ~5K), cutting the tokens you send to the LLM — and therefore your LLM bill — by orders of magnitude while enabling the LLM to operate safely inside a 128K window.

Short answer
- Use rlm_context_query for single-step documentation/code lookups and straightforward Q&A.
- Use rlm_decompose when the question is complex, multi-part, or must be split and orchestrated across many documents (near‑infinite context).

When to pick each (rules of thumb)
- rlm_context_query
  - One clear question: “Where is auth implemented?”, “What are the API endpoints?”, “How does X feature work?”
  - You expect a concise answer from a small-to-moderate slice of docs.
  - Fast, cheaper, and higher precision for documentation Q&A.
- rlm_decompose
  - Complex or large-scope questions: “Explain the complete authentication system across services”, “Audit all caching code and summarize patterns”.
  - The query naturally breaks into independent sub-questions or phases that can be run in parallel.
  - You need an execution plan (sub-queries, priorities, estimated tokens, dependencies) so you can run/track/merge results reliably.

Recommended workflow
1. Try rlm_context_query first for any doc/code question.
2. If the answer is incomplete, or the query is obviously multi-part or exceeds token/context limits, call rlm_decompose.
3. Use the sub-queries from rlm_decompose with rlm_multi_query (or individual rlm_context_query calls) and then synthesize the results.

Practical examples
- rlm_context_query: “Where is token validation implemented?” → one-shot lookup.
- rlm_decompose: “Document the entire auth flow (login, token generation, revocation, session storage, integrations)” → returns sub-queries, priorities, token estimates and a suggested sequence.

Notes
- rlm_decompose and rlm_multi_query are Power‑User / Pro+ features intended for orchestration of large/complex tasks.
- Always prefer the simpler direct tool first; escalate to decomposition when the direct query hits limits or the problem needs planning.

Short answer
- Use rlm_context_query for almost all documentation/code lookups — single-step semantic search, fast and token-efficient.
- Use rlm_decompose when a question is complex/large (multi-part, cross-file, or exceeds single-call context) and you need an execution plan (sub-queries, priorities, token budgets) before running retrievals.

When to prefer rlm_context_query
- You want a straightforward answer about how something works, where code lives, or a specific doc snippet.
- Query fits in a single semantic search / LLM pass.
- You want lowest latency and cost.
- Example: "Where is authentication handled?" or "List API endpoints for billing."

When to prefer rlm_decompose
- The problem is complex or broad (e.g., “Explain the complete authentication system across services”).
- You expect many interdependent sub-questions or very large docs that will overflow a single context window.
- You want to inspect/modify sub-queries, set per-subquery token budgets, or get an ordered execution plan.
- You plan to run multiple retrievals in parallel (rlm_multi_query) or synthesize many pieces into a final answer.
- Example: decompose into login flow, token generation, session storage, logout/revocation with priorities and estimated tokens.

Recommended workflow (best practice)
1. Try rlm_context_query first for the question.
2. If results are incomplete or the question is clearly multi-part/too large → call rlm_decompose to get sub-queries + plan.
3. Execute the sub-queries (rlm_multi_query or individual rlm_context_query calls) per the plan.
4. Synthesize results into the final answer.

Notes
- rlm_decompose adds orchestration overhead (more tokens/latency) — use it only when needed.
- rlm_decompose is a power-user / Pro+ tool intended for near‑infinite context or complex orchestration.

Answer:

- Use rlm_context_query when you have a straightforward documentation/code question that can be answered with a single context fetch. Examples: “Where is auth handled?”, “How does the payment flow work?”, “List API endpoints.” It's faster, cheaper, and usually more accurate for single-step retrieval.

- Use rlm_decompose when the question is complex, multi-part, or must span very large docs (near‑infinite context). rlm_decompose breaks the query into ordered sub-queries, returns an execution plan and token estimates, and is meant to be combined with rlm_multi_query/rlm_plan to run and synthesize results. Use it when you need:
  - a structured execution plan for a big audit or architecture explanation
  - dependence ordering across sub‑questions
  - to orchestrate many parallel/serial queries over large code/doc sets

Practical rule of thumb:
1. Try rlm_context_query first for documentation Q&A (single-step).
2. If the answer is incomplete or the question is multi-part/large, run rlm_decompose → inspect sub-queries → run rlm_multi_query or follow the suggested sequence to gather and synthesize results.

Examples:
- rlm_context_query: “What JWT claims are required in our auth tokens?”
- rlm_decompose: “Explain the complete authentication system” → returns sub-queries like “login flow”, “JWT generation/validation”, “session storage”, with dependencies and token estimates.

Note: rlm_decompose and its orchestration features are Pro+ (power-user) tools — use them only when the added planning/orchestration benefit outweighs the extra overhead.

Short answer: use keyword for exact/precise matches, semantic for conceptual/explanatory questions, and hybrid when you want the strengths of both.

Details and examples:
- Keyword
  - Best when you know exact names/terms, file paths, or need precise matches (e.g., “find function X”, error code lookup, API endpoint name).
  - Pros: fast, cheap, deterministic. Free tier.
  - Use cases from docs: “Looking for specific function”, “Error code lookup”, “API endpoint discovery”.

- Semantic
  - Best for conceptual, “how does X work?”, architecture, or when wording varies across docs (e.g., “How does authentication work?”, “explain the payment flow”).
  - Pros: finds related content even if wording differs.
  - Requires Pro+.

- Hybrid
  - Combines keyword precision with semantic recall — best when you want broad coverage plus exact hits (e.g., general documentation search, best practices, ambiguous queries).
  - Use when you aren’t sure which approach will work or when you need the highest-quality results.
  - Requires Pro+.

Quick mapping (from the docs):
- Looking for specific function → keyword
- “How does X work?” → semantic
- General documentation search → hybrid
- Error code lookup → keyword
- Architecture understanding → semantic
- API endpoint discovery → keyword
- Best practices search → hybrid

Operational tips:
- Always try rlm_context_query first for documentation/code Q&A (per Snipara workflow).
- If you already know the exact file path or need to edit files, use local tools instead.
- For cross-project implementation searches, use rlm_multi_project_query (team tool).

Use the mode that matches your intent — whether you need exact term matching, conceptual/architectural understanding, or a mix.

Quick summary
- keyword — exact, fast, cheap (good for precise lookups)
- semantic — concept-level, better for “how/why” and architecture
- hybrid — combines both; best general-purpose results

When to use each (with examples)
- keyword
  - Use when you know exact names/identifiers or need precise matches.
  - Examples: “find function named validateToken”, “error code 500-DB-CONN”, “list API endpoints.”
  - Why: deterministic, fast, works on Free plan.
- semantic
  - Use when you want concept similarity, design/architecture, or explanations.
  - Examples: “How does authentication work?”, “Where is configuration loaded?”, “Explain the deployment flow.”
  - Why: finds conceptually relevant docs even if wording differs; good for architecture and “how” questions. (Pro+)
- hybrid
  - Use for broad documentation searches or when you want the best of both.
  - Examples: “best practices for caching”, “search for rate-limit implementation across services”, “general docs about logging.”
  - Why: combines exact matching + semantic relevance — usually gives the highest-quality results. (Pro+)

Practical guidance
- Default to hybrid for most general/documentation searches if you have Pro+. It usually returns the best results.
- For quick, cheap lookups or when you know the exact token, use keyword.
- For conceptual questions, architecture, or when terms differ across docs, use semantic.
- Always try rlm_context_query first and set its mode to the choice above (keyword/semantic/hybrid).

Short answer: pick keyword for exact, lexical lookups; semantic for conceptual/architecture/explanations; hybrid for most ambiguous or broad documentation searches. (Keyword is Free; Semantic & Hybrid require Pro+.)

Recommendation matrix (quick):

- Keyword — use when you know exact identifiers or want precise matches
  - Good for: specific function names, config keys, error codes, API endpoint discovery, “where is X implemented?”
  - Why: fast, deterministic lexical matching
  - Plan: Free

- Semantic — use when you want conceptual understanding, design intent, or architecture-level answers
  - Good for: “How does X work?”, architecture overviews, why a pattern was chosen, high-level explanations
  - Why: embedding similarity surfaces relevant conceptual passages even if wording differs
  - Plan: Pro+

- Hybrid — use when you want the best of both (recommended default if available)
  - Good for: general documentation search, best practices, ambiguous queries, “find examples + docs”
  - Why: combines lexical precision with semantic breadth for higher-quality results
  - Plan: Pro+

Quick decision flow:
1. Know the exact term? → Keyword.
2. Need conceptual/explanatory content? → Semantic.
3. Unsure / broad search / best overall quality? → Hybrid (preferred if on Pro+).
4. No Pro+ access? → Use Keyword, or run multiple keyword queries to refine.

Tip: For documentation/code Q&A, always try rlm_context_query first (it supports these modes and is the primary tool for doc lookups).

All supported auth methods (what they look like and where they’re accepted)

- OAuth (Device Flow)
  - Header: Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_...
  - Flow: RFC 8628 device flow — user runs CLI, opens browser, enters code, server issues access + refresh tokens.
  - Where used: MCP endpoints (api.snipara.com/mcp/...) and other endpoints that accept Bearer tokens.
  - Local client behavior: tokens are stored in ~/.snipara/tokens.json by the CLI and the MCP client will prefer this file before falling back to an API key.
  - Server model: access & refresh tokens are stored as hashed values (accessTokenHash, refreshTokenHash); tokenPrefix (first 12 chars) is used for identification; scopes (e.g., "mcp:read mcp:write") are enforced; accessExpiresAt and refreshExpiresAt tracked.
  - Management: listable via GET /api/settings/tokens and revocable via DELETE /api/settings/tokens/[tokenId].

- API Key
  - Header: X-API-Key: rlm_...
  - Flow: static API key issued in Dashboard. There are project keys (preferred lookup) and team keys (fallback) — both stored hashed in DB (SHA‑256).
  - Where used: MCP endpoints accept either API key or OAuth; Inject API endpoints (www.snipara.com/api/projects/.../automation/inject) require X-API-Key.
  - Server model: incoming key is hashed and compared to DB; logs only show prefix/first chars for safety.

How they differ in security (trade‑offs)

- Lifespan & revocation
  - OAuth: short‑lived access tokens + refresh tokens. Easier to expire/revoke centrally and to grant limited lifetime. Refresh tokens can extend sessions but are also tracked and revocable.
  - API keys: usually long‑lived (static). If leaked, they remain valid until rotated/revoked.

- Scope & least privilege
  - OAuth: supports scopes (fine‑grained read/write limits) so you can grant only the permissions the client needs.
  - API keys: typically grant whatever the key was created for (project or team level); less granular unless the dashboard supports scoped keys.

- User interaction & leakage risk
  - OAuth device flow: interactive sign‑in in browser — no manual copying of long secrets into CLI workflows; reduces accidental exposures and supports per‑client tokens.
  - API keys: convenient for automation but riskier if embedded in code/config or checked into repos. Must be stored securely (env vars, secrets manager).

- Storage & server protection
  - Both tokens and API keys are stored hashed in the server DB (access/refresh tokens hashed; API keys SHA‑256 hashed), which limits server‑side leakage risk.
  - Locally, OAuth tokens are stored in ~/.snipara/tokens.json — compromise of that file exposes the client’s tokens. Removing or backing up that file (rm or mv) forces use of API key instead.

- Attack surface & best practice
  - OAuth is generally more secure for interactive users: scoped, short‑lived, revocable, less manual secret handling.
  - API keys are practical for non‑interactive automation but must be rotated frequently, restricted to minimal privileges, stored in secrets managers or env vars, and never committed.
  - Both should be sent over HTTPS and protected from logs; server shows only prefix info in logs/UIs.

Quick operational recommendations
- Use OAuth device flow for user-facing CLI login and when you want scoped, revocable access.
- Use API keys for automated services where non‑interactive auth is required — store them in a secrets manager and rotate regularly.
- Revoke or rotate keys/tokens if suspected compromise; use GET/DELETE token endpoints for OAuth tokens.
- If you want to force API‑key use on a machine, remove ~/.snipara/tokens.json (or move it to a backup).

Short answer — there are two main authentication methods:

- API keys (rlm_...):
  - Two flavors: project API keys (single-project) and team API keys (team-wide).
  - Sent in requests as X-API-Key: rlm_...
  - Server hashes the key (SHA‑256) and looks it up in the ApiKey table; only prefixes or first 8 chars are shown in UI/logs.
  - Used by both MCP and Inject endpoints (Inject requires X-API-Key; MCP accepts either API key or OAuth).
  - Best for server-to-server automation and non-interactive use.

- OAuth Device‑flow tokens (snipara_at_... and refresh tokens):
  - User authenticates via a browser/device code flow (RFC 8628); tokens sent as Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_...
  - Access and refresh tokens are stored/represented as hashes in the DB; tokens are also stored locally in ~/.snipara/tokens.json by clients.
  - Tokens have scopes (e.g., mcp:read mcp:write), expiration, and can be revoked via the dashboard or API.
  - Preferred for interactive CLI/desktop authentication where you don’t want to copy/paste API keys.

How they differ in security (summary):

- Lifetime and revocation
  - OAuth: short-lived access tokens, refresh tokens, can expire and be revoked; easier to rotate and limit lifetime.
  - API keys: typically long‑lived until you regenerate/revoke them; require manual rotation.

- Scope and least privilege
  - OAuth: supports explicit scopes (granular permissions).
  - API keys: grant project- or team-level access; less granular than per-scope OAuth tokens (though metadata can include access_level).

- Exposure risk
  - OAuth device flow avoids copying raw secrets into the CLI/browser flow, reducing accidental exposure.
  - API keys are bearer secrets; if leaked they grant access until revoked/rotated.

- Storage and logging
  - Both token types are hashed in the DB (good). The system logs/display only prefixes to reduce full-secret exposure.
  - Local OAuth tokens are stored in ~/.snipara/tokens.json — if an attacker gains access to that file they can use the tokens; you can remove it (rm or mv) to force API key usage.
  - API keys sent in headers are exposed to any middleware that logs full headers unless truncated; UI shows only prefixes.

- Use-case suitability
  - OAuth: better for user-level access, finer-grained scope, interactive login, safer for end-users.
  - API keys: simpler for automated services, CI/CD, and server-to-server calls; but require careful rotation and storage.

- Endpoint support & precedence
  - MCP endpoints accept either OAuth Bearer or X-API-Key; Inject API expects X-API-Key.
  - The client checks ~/.snipara/tokens.json first (OAuth) then falls back to API key (env), so removing tokens.json forces API key auth.

Recommendations (brief)
- Use OAuth device flow for interactive CLI/desktop use and when you want scoped, short‑lived credentials.
- Use project-scoped API keys for automation where OAuth is impractical; restrict to the minimum project/team and rotate regularly.
- Store secrets securely, avoid pasting keys, rotate/revoke compromised secrets, and prefer least privilege.

Authentication options

- API keys
  - Header: X-API-Key: rlm_...
  - Types: project API key (scoped to a single project) and team API key (broader access to all projects in a team).
  - Server handling: client-sent key is SHA‑256 hashed and compared to hashes in the ApiKey table.
  - Visibility/storage: only initial chars are shown in UI/logs (first 8 chars); full key must be kept secret by the client.
  - Where accepted: MCP endpoints and Inject API accept X-API-Key. MCP server will fall back to an API key if no OAuth token is found.
  - Management: create/rotate in dashboard; invalid API key errors instruct key regeneration.
  - Security characteristics: long‑lived bearer credential, broad power if leaked (team keys more powerful). Good for machine-to-machine scenarios but must be protected and rotated.

- OAuth device‑flow tokens (OAuth tokens)
  - Header: Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_...
  - Flow: RFC 8628 device flow (CLI shows code and URL; user authorizes in browser).
  - Token model: access and refresh tokens are stored as hashes (OAuthToken record includes tokenPrefix, scope, accessExpiresAt, refreshExpiresAt).
  - Scopes: tokens can carry scoped permissions (e.g., mcp:read mcp:write); server enforces scopes.
  - Expiry & refresh: access tokens expire (expires_in in poll response) and can be refreshed using refresh tokens; revocation supported (DELETE /api/settings/tokens/[id]).
  - Where accepted: MCP server supports OAuth bearer tokens; Inject API uses API keys only.
  - Security characteristics: short‑lived, scoped tokens with refresh and revocation — better security and least privilege compared with long‑lived API keys; avoids copying full API keys into CLI.

Other operational details
- Priority on MCP: server first checks for a stored OAuth token (e.g., ~/.snipara/tokens.json) then falls back to API key from environment.
- You can force API‑key auth by removing or moving ~/.snipara/tokens.json so the client won’t present OAuth tokens.
- Validation differences: API keys validated by hashing and DB lookup; OAuth tokens validated by DB lookup and expiry/scope checks.
- Revocation: OAuth tokens can be listed (GET /api/settings/tokens) and revoked (DELETE /api/settings/tokens/[id]). API keys are managed via the dashboard.

Which to choose (short guidance)
- Prefer OAuth device flow for CLI/interactive use: shorter lived, scoped, revocable, and avoids exposing long-lived keys.
- Use project API keys when a service needs stable programmatic access limited to one project.
- Avoid using team API keys unless you need cross-project access (they’re higher privilege and riskier if leaked).
- Always rotate credentials, limit scopes, and store keys/tokens securely.

Short answer
- The total token budget is split between project docs and shared-context docs (shared is allocated by category). Project docs are filled first, then shared docs by category priority. Documents are included until each category’s allocated tokens are exhausted; anything beyond the category budget is marked as excluded.

How it works (details)
1. Budget buckets
   - Conceptually shared categories have priority tiers: MANDATORY > BEST_PRACTICES > GUIDELINES > REFERENCE.
   - The system uses a default allocation table (can be overridden):
     - project: 0.40 (40% of total budget)
     - MANDATORY: 0.25 (25%)
     - BEST_PRACTICES: 0.20 (20%)
     - GUIDELINES: 0.10 (10%)
     - REFERENCE: 0.05 (5%)
   - Note: higher-level docs also describe a conceptual 40/30/20/10 split for shared categories; the runtime code uses the DEFAULT_ALLOCATION above, and you can pass a customAllocation to change it.

2. Query ordering
   - When running rlm_context_query, shared context is queried first (you can set shared_context_budget_percent), then local project scope is queried with the remaining budget. Shared docs are merged from linked collections and allocated according to the category budgets.

3. Allocation algorithm (allocateTokenBudget)
   - The code groups docs into project and shared-by-category.
   - It computes per-bucket budgets = floor(totalBudget * allocation[bucket]).
   - It allocates project docs first using the project budget.
   - Then it allocates each shared category (MANDATORY, BEST_PRACTICES, GUIDELINES, REFERENCE) using that category’s budget.
   - Within each bucket, docs are sorted by relevanceScore (if present) and consumed sequentially. A doc is included only if tokensUsed + doc.tokenCount <= bucketBudget. Otherwise it’s marked allocated: false / "exceeded budget".

4. Overrides and parameters
   - You can pass customAllocation to allocateTokenBudget to change the percentages.
   - rlm_shared_context and rlm_context_query accept parameters (categories, max_tokens, shared_context_budget_percent, include_project_docs) to influence what is pulled and how budgets are allocated.

5. Hard limits and enforcement
   - Collection-level hard limits exist (e.g., MAX_TOKENS_PER_COLLECTION). enforceCollectionLimits truncates a collection to fit the max tokens per collection.
   - Documents carry precomputed tokenCount; allocation uses those counts.

Example
- If totalBudget = 10,000 and using the runtime DEFAULT_ALLOCATION:
  - project budget = 4,000 tokens
  - MANDATORY = 2,500
  - BEST_PRACTICES = 2,000
  - GUIDELINES = 1,000
  - REFERENCE = 500
  The allocator will pick project docs up to 4,000 tokens, then pick MANDATORY docs up to 2,500 tokens (highest priority), then BEST_PRACTICES, etc., stopping when each bucket is exhausted.

Bottom line
- Token budget allocation guarantees project docs get a guaranteed slice, then enforces shared-category priorities. You can customize the distribution, and the allocator includes docs in order of relevance until each bucket’s token limit is reached, with hard collection-level caps enforced.

Short answer
- When you run a context query, the system splits the token budget between project docs and shared context, then within shared context it divides tokens across categories (MANDATORY, BEST_PRACTICES, GUIDELINES, REFERENCE). Documents are selected in priority/relevance order until each category’s budget is exhausted.

How it works (step‑by‑step)
1. Shared context is queried first (so its rules can influence results) and project docs are queried with the remaining tokens.
2. The overall token pool is split according to an allocation object. The runtime uses a DEFAULT_ALLOCATION but it can be overridden:
   - Default allocation used by allocateTokenBudget (code): project = 0.40, MANDATORY = 0.25, BEST_PRACTICES = 0.20, GUIDELINES = 0.10, REFERENCE = 0.05.
   - (Docs also show the higher‑level/documentation percentages often used for teams: MANDATORY 40%, BEST_PRACTICES 30%, GUIDELINES 20%, REFERENCE 10 — those are conceptual defaults and can be reflected by configuration.)
3. Allocation algorithm:
   - Compute projectBudget = floor(totalBudget * allocation.project) and allocate project documents first.
   - For each shared category, compute categoryBudget = floor(totalBudget * allocation[category]) and allocate documents for that category.
   - Within a group, docs are sorted by relevanceScore (falling back to stored priority) and added until the budget for that group is used. Docs that don’t fit are marked allocated=false (exceeded budget).
4. Results return documents with allocated (true/false) and an allocationReason (e.g., "project allocation", "MANDATORY allocation", or "exceeded budget").

Customization and parameters
- You can pass a customAllocation to allocateTokenBudget (or set shared_context_budget_percent in rlm_context_query) to change the split between project vs shared and per-category percentages.
- rlm_shared_context supports filtering by categories and max_tokens so you can control how much shared content is returned.

Hard limits and safety
- Collection-level limits are enforced (e.g., MAX_TOKENS_PER_COLLECTION) and collections may be truncated to fit those limits.
- Other limits include MAX_COLLECTIONS_PER_PROJECT, MAX_DOCS_PER_COLLECTION, and MIN_RELEVANCE_THRESHOLD.

Small numeric example (using the code defaults)
- totalBudget = 4000:
  - projectBudget = floor(4000 * 0.40) = 1600
  - MANDATORY = floor(4000 * 0.25) = 1000
  - BEST_PRACTICES = 800
  - GUIDELINES = 400
  - REFERENCE = 200
Documents are pulled into those buckets in relevance/priority order until each bucket is full.

Bottom line
Shared context is included up front and is given a reserved portion of the token budget split by category; project docs get their own reserved portion. The allocation is deterministic, relevance-aware, and configurable, and collection hard limits are enforced to keep the returned context within bounds.

Short answer
- When you run a context query, tokens are split between shared context (team collections) and project docs. Shared context is loaded first, then local project docs use the remaining token budget.
- The shared portion is itself split by category priority (MANDATORY, BEST_PRACTICES, GUIDELINES, REFERENCE), and documents are included until each category’s sub-budget is exhausted.

How it actually works (steps & algorithm)
1. Overall split
   - The system first decides how many tokens go to shared context vs. project context. This split is configurable (examples in the docs show 30% shared / 70% project and elsewhere 40% shared / 60% project). The token-budget implementation default is:
     - project: 40% (project docs)
     - shared total: 60% (split among shared categories)
   - You can override the split via parameters (e.g., shared_context_budget_percent / include_shared_context or by passing a customAllocation to allocateTokenBudget).

2. Shared-category allocation
   - The shared portion is broken down into category buckets with priority-weighted percentages. Two places show the category weights you’ll encounter:
     - User-facing category distribution (commonly shown in docs and examples): MANDATORY 40%, BEST_PRACTICES 30%, GUIDELINES 20%, REFERENCE 10%
     - Implementation DEFAULT_ALLOCATION in token-budget.ts (used by allocateTokenBudget): MANDATORY 25%, BEST_PRACTICES 20%, GUIDELINES 10%, REFERENCE 5% (these, combined with project:40%, sum to 100%)
   - In either case, MANDATORY has the highest priority. The exact percent used depends on the configuration/defaults in your deployment.

3. Per-bucket selection
   - For each bucket (project first, then each shared category), the code:
     - Computes the bucket budget = floor(totalBudget * allocation[bucket])
     - Sorts documents by relevanceScore (if present), then by priority
     - Includes documents in order until the bucket budget would be exceeded
     - Documents that don’t fit are marked “not allocated”

4. Hard limits & enforcement
   - There are hard limits enforced (e.g., MAX_COLLECTIONS_PER_PROJECT, MAX_TOKENS_PER_COLLECTION, MAX_DOCS_PER_COLLECTION). If a collection exceeds MAX_TOKENS_PER_COLLECTION, docs are truncated to fit the limit.

Practical notes
- Shared context is queried first (so MANDATORY rules and high-priority shared docs are seen before project docs).
- You can filter which shared categories to request (e.g., only MANDATORY and BEST_PRACTICES) to reduce shared consumption.
- To change behavior, use the rlm_context_query parameters (include_shared_context, shared_context_budget_percent) or pass customAllocation to allocateTokenBudget.

Instead, it provides the primitives you need to build one: session/shared context APIs, SSE/transport hooks (MCP SSE), and integrations (VS Code extension, Continue.dev example). Use those plus a CRDT (Yjs/Automerge) or Live Share for the live editing layer and use Snipara to index/share/update the optimized context in real time.

   - So the supported pattern is: handle realtime editing with an editor/collab layer and synchronize context through Snipara’s MCP / shared context APIs.

2) Typical architecture (recommended)
   - Clients (browser editors or VS Code) run a CRDT engine (Yjs or Automerge) to handle concurrent edits locally.
   - A collaboration server (or P2P WebRTC) persists CRDT state, author metadata, access control, and emits real‑time updates to other clients via WebSocket or SSE.
   - When edits reach a stable checkpoint (or on each change if you want immediate reindexing):
     - Push a compacted/summary version of the document to Snipara via the shared/session context API (rlm_shared_context / rlm_inject) or through your MCP integration so Snipara can reindex or update the optimized context.
     - Optionally call rlm_context_query to refresh optimized context for active queries.
   - Use Snipara’s SSE/MCP transport to receive context updates or to integrate with third‑party editors (example: Continue.dev SSE integration).

3) Concrete building blocks
   - Collab engine: Yjs (with y-websocket) or Automerge.
   - Persistence/service: Node/Go server with a CRDT store (LevelDB, Postgres, Redis).
   - Real‑time transport: WebSocket (preferred) or SSE.
   - Snipara hooks:
     - Use the MCP SSE transport for streaming/integration (docs show how to configure a MCP server with SSE and the X-API-Key header).
     - Use rlm_shared_context and rlm_inject (listed tools) to share session summaries/contexts across the team so other Snipara clients get the updated context.
   - Editor integrations:
     - For VS Code: extend the existing extension to sync CRDT state or call Live Share APIs (recommended if you want a quick path).
     - For web: integrate Yjs + CodeMirror/Monaco.

4) Example flow (short)
   - A edits → Yjs updates locally → sends update to collab server → server persists & broadcasts to other clients → server compacts/creates summary → server calls Snipara shared-context/inject API (or reindexes) → Snipara optimizes context; downstream queries get updated context via rlm_context_query or SSE notifications.

   - Use CRDTs (Yjs) rather than naive diffs to avoid conflicts.

Short answer: Snipara doesn’t ship a built‑in WYSIWYG real‑time collaborative editor. Instead you implement collaboration by pairing Snipara’s realtime/streaming MCP surface (SSE + session/context APIs) and the existing editor/collab tooling you already use (VS Code Live Share, Google Docs, Notion, etc.). Below are recommended patterns and concrete steps.

Recommended options

- For developer teams (code + docs)
  - Use the snipara-vscode extension + a collaborative editor (VS Code Live Share). The extension exposes RLM queries, session context management and the Inject/Show/Clear Context commands so collaborators can share the same Snipara session context while editing.
- For web apps/docs that need true realtime updates
  - Use the MCP SSE transport to stream context updates into Snipara (the Continue.dev example shows the SSE transport). When your editor/web UI emits changes, push those updates to Snipara via SSE or the rlm_inject/session APIs so all collaborators and agents see the updated session context.
- For integrations/legacy tools
  - Proxy updates through the planned @rlmsaas/mcp-bridge (universal bridge is listed as TODO) or build a small webhook that calls the Snipara session APIs.

Concrete implementation sketch

1. Store canonical content in git (or your CMS) so changes are versioned and re-indexable.
2. Pick your collaboration surface:
   - VS Code: use Live Share + snipara-vscode
   - Web editor: use your editor’s realtime layer (WebSocket/OT) or Google Docs/Notion for text
3. On each save/important edit:
   - Push the new content to Snipara so the shared session sees it. Two approaches:
     - Stream via MCP SSE (example transport config from the docs):
       { "mcpServers": [ { "name": "rlm", "transport": { "type":"sse", "url":"https://api.rlmsaas.com/v1/PROJECT_ID/mcp/sse", "headers": { "X-API-Key":"{{RLM_API_KEY}}" } } } ] }
     - Or call the session APIs (rlm_inject / rlm_context / rlm_clear_context) to inject the changed content into the session context.
4. When collaborators ask questions or run the assistant:
   - Use rlm_context_query to fetch an optimized, token‑budgeted context for the question (so everyone gets the same optimized view).
   - Use rlm_ask for quick queries where budget/config isn’t needed.
5. Reindexing / persistence
   - Ensure server-side hooks reindex or re‑push content when the canonical source (git/CMS) changes so historical searches return fresh content.

Caveats & notes

- There is no built-in Snipara “live-editor” UI — Snipara is optimized for context retrieval, session context sharing, and streaming updates. Real‑time editing UI should remain in your editor/CMS; Snipara consumes and serves that context to LLM sessions and teammates.
- The universal bridge (@rlmsaas/mcp-bridge) is planned but not yet complete — if you need a cross-tool MCP proxy today you’ll need a small custom proxy/webhook.
- For help implementing a specific flow (VS Code Live Share + snipara-vscode, or a web editor + SSE webhook), tell me which editor/CMS you use and whether you want example code for the webhook or sample VS Code extension config — I can produce step‑by‑step code.
